category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 02/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text: Part A: General Aptitude (Q1–15). Marks: +2 correct, −0.5 incorrect. Attempt any 12.

questiontext.text: A and B invest Rs. 3000 and Rs. 5000 in a business. At the end of the year they make a profit of Rs. 4800. What is B's share of the profit?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Rs. 3000

Ratio of investment = 3000:5000 = 3:5. B's share = (5/8)×4800 = Rs. 3000.

questiontext.text: Two trains of lengths 120 m and 180 m travel in opposite directions at speeds of 50 km/h and 70 km/h respectively. How long will they take to cross each other?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 6 seconds

Relative speed = 50+70 = 120 km/h = 120×5/18 = 100/3 m/s. Total length = 120+180 = 300 m. Time = 300÷(100/3) = 9 s.

Wait — 300/(100/3) = 900/100 = 9 s. Answer: 9 seconds.

questiontext.text: What is the HCF of 72, 108 and 180?
Book : Library :: Painting : ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Gallery

Books are stored and displayed in a library; paintings are stored and displayed in a gallery.

questiontext.text: The ratio of ages of Priya and Meena is 3:5. After 6 years the ratio will be 3:4. What is Priya's present age?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 6 years

Let ages be 3x and 5x. (3x+6)/(5x+6)=3/4 ⟹ 12x+24=15x+18 ⟹ 3x=6 ⟹ x=2. Priya = 3×2 = 6.

questiontext.text: Find the next term: 1, 8, 27, 64, 125, ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) 216

Pattern: n³. Next: 6³=216.

name.text: P02 Q7 - Discount on marked price

questiontext.text: A shopkeeper marks an article 40% above cost price and gives a 10% discount. What is the profit percentage?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 26%

Let CP=100. MP=140. SP=140×0.9=126. Profit=26%.

questiontext.text: How many 3-letter words (with or without meaning) can be formed from the letters of the word MATHS using each letter at most once?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 60

MATHS has 5 distinct letters. ⁵P₃ = 5×4×3 = 60.

questiontext.text: A clock shows the time 9:30. What time does its mirror image show?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 2:30

Mirror image of a clock: subtract the clock time from 12:00 (or 11:60). 11:60 − 9:30 = 2:30.

questiontext.text: A trapezium has parallel sides of lengths 8 cm and 14 cm, and a height of 6 cm. What is its area?
Ravi introduces a man saying "He is the son of the woman who is the mother of the husband of my mother." How is the man related to Ravi?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Uncle

Mother of husband of Ravi's mother = Ravi's paternal grandmother. Son of Ravi's paternal grandmother = Ravi's father or uncle. The man introduced is distinct from Ravi's father, so he is Ravi's uncle (father's brother).

questiontext.text: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability that the sum of the numbers is 8?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 5/36

Pairs giving sum 8: (2,6),(3,5),(4,4),(5,3),(6,2) = 5 outcomes. P = 5/36.

name.text: P02 Q13 - Odd one out planets

questiontext.text: Which is the odd one out? Mars, Venus, Moon, Jupiter, Saturn

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Moon

Mars, Venus, Jupiter, and Saturn are planets. Moon is a natural satellite.

name.text: P02 Q14 - CI vs SI difference

questiontext.text: The difference between CI and SI on Rs. 10000 at 10% p.a. for 2 years is:
All cats are animals. All animals are living beings. Conclusion: All cats are living beings. The argument is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Valid

This is a valid syllogism: if all A are B, and all B are C, then all A are C.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 02/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16–40). Marks: +3 correct, −1 incorrect. Attempt any 20.

questiontext.text: \(\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 0}\frac{\sin(3x)}{5x}\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 3/5

Using lim_{x→0} sin(kx)/(kx)=1: lim sin(3x)/(5x) = (3/5)·lim sin(3x)/(3x) = 3/5.

name.text: P02 Q17 - Taylor series of cos x

questiontext.text: The Taylor series of \(\cos x\) about \(x=0\) is:
By Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem, for \(f(x)=x^2\) on \([1,3]\), there exists \(c\in(1,3)\) such that \(f'(c)=\):
\(\displaystyle\int_0^1 x e^x\,dx\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 1

Integration by parts: ∫xe^x dx = xe^x − e^x + C. Evaluating: [xe^x − e^x]₀¹ = (e−e)−(0−1) = 0+1 = 1.

name.text: P02 Q20 - Monotone function Riemann integrable

questiontext.text: Every monotone bounded function on \([a,b]\) is Riemann integrable. This is because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) it has at most countably many discontinuities

A monotone function can only have jump discontinuities, and the set of jump discontinuities is at most countable, hence has measure zero. By Lebesgue's criterion, f is Riemann integrable.

answer.text: it has at most countably many discontinuities (measure-zero set)

name.text: P02 Q21 - Eigenvalues of nilpotent matrix

questiontext.text: If \(A\) is a nilpotent matrix (i.e., \(A^k=0\) for some \(k>0\)), then all eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 0

If λ is an eigenvalue of A with eigenvector v, then Aᵏv = λᵏv = 0·v = 0 ⟹ λᵏ=0 ⟹ λ=0.

name.text: P02 Q22 - Similar matrices same eigenvalues

questiontext.text: If matrices \(A\) and \(B\) are similar (\(B=P^{-1}AP\) for some invertible \(P\)), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) A and B have the same eigenvalues

Similar matrices have the same characteristic polynomial (det(B−λI)=det(P⁻¹AP−λI)=det(P⁻¹(A−λI)P)=det(A−λI)), hence the same eigenvalues.

questiontext.text: In an inner product space, the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states that for vectors \(u\) and \(v\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) |⟨u,v⟩| ≤ ||u|| ||v||

The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality: |⟨u,v⟩|² ≤ ⟨u,u⟩⟨v,v⟩, i.e., |⟨u,v⟩| ≤ ||u||·||v||.

name.text: P02 Q24 - Isomorphism Z6 to Z2xZ3

questiontext.text: Which of the following groups is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}_6\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ℤ₂ × ℤ₃

By CRT, since gcd(2,3)=1, ℤ₆ ≅ ℤ₂ × ℤ₃. Both are cyclic of order 6. ℤ₂×ℤ₂ has order 4. S₃ is non-abelian.

questiontext.text: Which of the following polynomials is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) x³ − 2

By Eisenstein with p=2: x³−2 is irreducible over ℚ. x²−4 = (x−2)(x+2), x²−1=(x−1)(x+1) are reducible. x⁴−1 factors as (x−1)(x+1)(x²+1).

questiontext.text: By Liouville's theorem, a bounded entire function is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) constant

Liouville's theorem: if f is entire (holomorphic on all of ℂ) and bounded, then f is constant.

name.text: P02 Q27 - Laurent series essential singularity

questiontext.text: The function \(f(z) = e^{1/z}\) has at \(z=0\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) an essential singularity

e^{1/z} = Σ 1/(n! z^n) has infinitely many negative powers in its Laurent expansion, so z=0 is an essential singularity.

questiontext.text: Which of the following is TRUE about topological spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Every path-connected space is connected

Path-connectedness implies connectedness. The converse is false (the topologist's sine curve is connected but not path-connected).

answer.text: Connected and path-connected are equivalent for all spaces

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is Hausdorff if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) every pair of distinct points can be separated by open sets

Hausdorff (T₂) means: for any x≠y, there exist open U∋x and V∋y with U∩V=∅.

answer.text: every pair of distinct points can be separated by disjoint open sets
every sequence has a unique limit

name.text: P02 Q30 - Second order ODE repeated root

questiontext.text: The general solution of \(y'' - 6y' + 9y = 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) (c₁ + c₂x)e³ˣ

Characteristic equation: r²−6r+9=(r−3)²=0. Repeated root r=3. General solution: (c₁+c₂x)e³ˣ.

questiontext.text: A function \(u(x,y)\) satisfying \(u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0\) is called:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) harmonic

Solutions to Laplace's equation ∇²u=0 are called harmonic functions. They are infinitely differentiable and satisfy the mean value property.

name.text: P02 Q32 - Wave equation solution d'Alembert

questiontext.text: The solution of the wave equation \(u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}\) with initial conditions \(u(x,0)=f(x)\) and \(u_t(x,0)=0\) is given by d'Alembert's formula:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) u(x,t) = [f(x+ct)+f(x−ct)]/2

D'Alembert's solution with zero initial velocity: u = [f(x+ct)+f(x−ct)]/2.

name.text: P02 Q33 - Variance of uniform distribution

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim\text{Uniform}[a,b]\), then \(\text{Var}(X)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) (b−a)²/12

For Uniform[a,b]: E[X]=(a+b)/2, Var(X)=(b−a)²/12.

questiontext.text: Markov's inequality states that for a non-negative random variable \(X\) and \(a>0\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) P(X ≥ a) ≤ E[X]/a

Markov's inequality: P(X≥a) ≤ E[X]/a for X≥0.

questiontext.text: The Central Limit Theorem states that for iid random variables \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2<\infty\), as \(n\to\infty\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) (X̄ − μ)/(σ/√n) → N(0,1) in distribution

The CLT: the standardised sample mean converges in distribution to the standard normal.

questiontext.text: The Baire Category Theorem states that a complete metric space:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) is not a countable union of nowhere-dense sets

Baire's theorem: in a complete metric space, the intersection of countably many dense open sets is dense; equivalently, the space is not meagre (first category).

answer.text: cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets

questiontext.text: The Banach Contraction Mapping Principle states that a contraction on a complete metric space:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) has a unique fixed point

If T: X→X satisfies d(Tx,Ty)≤kd(x,y) for k∈[0,1) on a complete metric space, then T has a unique fixed point.

answer.text: may have infinitely many fixed points
has at least two fixed points

name.text: P02 Q38 - Fundamental theorem of algebra

questiontext.text: The Fundamental Theorem of Algebra states that every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) has at least one complex root

FTA: every non-constant polynomial in ℂ[x] has at least one root in ℂ. Consequently, degree-n polynomial has exactly n roots (counted with multiplicity).

answer.text: has at least one complex root
has at least one real root
has exactly as many roots as its degree over \(\mathbb{R}\)

name.text: P02 Q39 - Fundamental group of circle

questiontext.text: The fundamental group \(\pi_1(S^1)\) of the circle \(S^1\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ℤ

π₁(S¹)≅ℤ, generated by the loop that winds once around the circle. The winding number is the isomorphism.

questiontext.text: The exponential distribution with parameter \(\lambda\) is the unique continuous distribution with the memoryless property. This means:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) P(X > s+t | X > s) = P(X > t) for all s,t > 0

Memoryless: given that you have already waited s, the additional waiting time has the same distribution.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 02/Part C - Advanced Mathematics

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q41–55). MSQ: +4 full credit, +2 partial, −1 wrong. Attempt any 10.

name.text: P02 Q41 MSQ - Continuity and differentiability

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about functions \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Differentiability implies continuity. (2) FALSE: |x| is continuous but not differentiable at 0. (3) TRUE: A function with bounded derivative is Lipschitz and hence uniformly continuous. (4) TRUE: Continuous function on closed bounded interval is uniformly continuous.

answer.text: If \(f\) is differentiable at \(x_0\), then \(f\) is continuous at \(x_0\).
If \(f\) has bounded derivative on \(\mathbb{R}\), then \(f\) is uniformly continuous.
A continuous function on a closed bounded interval \([a,b]\) is uniformly continuous.

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about finite-dimensional vector spaces over \(\mathbb{R}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Any two bases have the same cardinality (dimension). (2) TRUE: Dimension theorem. (3) TRUE: Linear maps between same-dimensional spaces — injective ⟺ surjective ⟺ bijective. (4) TRUE: Every subspace has a complementary subspace.

answer.text: Any two bases of a finite-dimensional vector space have the same number of elements.
For a linear map \(T:V\to W\), \(\dim(\ker T)+\dim(\mathrm{Im}\,T)=\dim V\).
A linear map from \(\mathbb{R}^n\) to \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is injective if and only if it is surjective.
Every subspace \(W\) of \(V\) has a complementary subspace \(W'\) with \(V=W\oplus W'\).

name.text: P02 Q43 MSQ - Abstract algebra quotient groups

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about quotient groups and homomorphisms?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: First Isomorphism Theorem. (2) TRUE: The kernel is always a normal subgroup. (3) FALSE: The image of a homomorphism need not be a normal subgroup of the codomain (only a subgroup). (4) TRUE: Second Isomorphism Theorem.

question.single: falsetrueabc If \(\phi:G\to H\) is a group homomorphism, then \(G/\ker\phi\cong\phi(G)\). The kernel of any group homomorphism is a normal subgroup. The image of a homomorphism \(\phi:G\to H\) is always normal in \(H\). If \(N\trianglelefteq G\) and \(H\leq G\), then \(H/(H\cap N)\cong HN/N\) (Second Isomorphism Theorem).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about analytic (holomorphic) functions on an open set \(U\subseteq\mathbb{C}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Open Mapping Theorem. (2) TRUE: Maximum Modulus Principle. (3) TRUE: Analytic = power series expansion at each point. (4) TRUE: Isolated singularity with bounded limit is removable.

answer.text: A non-constant holomorphic function on a connected open set is an open map (Open Mapping Theorem).
A non-constant holomorphic function on a bounded domain cannot attain its maximum modulus in the interior.
Every holomorphic function on an open set \(U\) can be represented as a convergent power series locally.
If \(f\) is holomorphic on \(0<|z-a|<r\) and \(\lim_{z\to a}f(z)\) exists finitely, then the singularity at \(z=a\) is removable.

name.text: P02 Q45 MSQ - Metric space properties

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about metric spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (2),(3),(4)

(1) FALSE: A complete metric space need not be compact (e.g., ℝ is complete but not compact). (2) TRUE: Compact metric spaces are complete and totally bounded. (3) TRUE: A complete totally bounded metric space is compact. (4) TRUE: Every compact metric space is separable.

answer.text: Every complete metric space is compact.
Every compact metric space is complete and totally bounded.
A metric space is compact if and only if it is complete and totally bounded.
Every compact metric space is separable (has a countable dense subset).

name.text: P02 Q46 MSQ - Integration and differentiation

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Lebesgue integration?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Every Riemann integrable function is Lebesgue integrable with the same value. (2) TRUE: Lebesgue integral extends Riemann integral. (3) TRUE: DCT. (4) TRUE: For integrable f, F(x)=∫_a^x f(t)dt is absolutely continuous and F'=f a.e.

answer.text: Every Riemann integrable bounded function on \([a,b]\) is also Lebesgue integrable with the same value.
The Lebesgue integral extends the Riemann integral.
If \(f_n\to f\) pointwise a.e. and \(|f_n|\leq g\in L^1\), then \(\int f_n\to\int f\).
If \(f\in L^1[a,b]\), then \(F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt\) is absolutely continuous and \(F'=f\) a.e.

name.text: P02 Q47 MSQ - PDE boundary value

questiontext.text: Consider the Dirichlet problem: \(\Delta u = 0\) in a bounded domain \(\Omega\), \(u=g\) on \(\partial\Omega\). Which are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: The Dirichlet problem has at most one solution (maximum principle). (2) TRUE: If a solution exists it is unique. (3) FALSE: Not every continuous g gives a solution (requires sufficient regularity of domain). (4) TRUE: Harmonic functions satisfy the mean value property.

answer.text: The solution to the Dirichlet problem is unique if it exists.
Uniqueness follows from the maximum principle for harmonic functions.
For any continuous \(g\) on \(\partial\Omega\), the Dirichlet problem always has a solution.
A harmonic function satisfies the mean value property.

name.text: P02 Q48 MSQ - Algebraic topology homotopy

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about homotopy and fundamental groups?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: π₁(S¹)≅ℤ. (2) TRUE: π₁(Sⁿ)=0 for n≥2. (3) TRUE: π₁(T²)≅ℤ×ℤ (torus). (4) TRUE: Homotopy equivalent spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups.

answer.text: Homotopy equivalent spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups.

name.text: P02 Q49 MSQ - Galois theory basics

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about field extensions and Galois theory?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: [ℚ(√2):ℚ]=2. (2) TRUE: A Galois extension satisfies |Gal(K/F)|=[K:F]. (3) FALSE: Gal(ℚ(√2)/ℚ)≅ℤ₂, not ℤ₄. (4) TRUE: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory.

answer.text: There is a bijection between subgroups of \(\mathrm{Gal}(K/F)\) and intermediate fields between \(F\) and \(K\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about bounded linear operators on Banach spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Open Mapping Theorem (surjective bounded linear operator is open). (2) TRUE: Closed Graph Theorem. (3) TRUE: Uniform Boundedness Principle (Banach-Steinhaus). (4) TRUE: Hahn-Banach allows extension of bounded linear functionals.

answer.text: A surjective bounded linear operator between Banach spaces is an open map.
A linear operator with a closed graph between Banach spaces is bounded (Closed Graph Theorem).
If a family of bounded linear operators is pointwise bounded, it is uniformly bounded (Banach-Steinhaus).
Every bounded linear functional on a subspace can be extended to the whole Banach space (Hahn-Banach).

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) be iid with density \(f(x;\theta)\). Which of the following are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: MLE is asymptotically efficient under regularity. (2) FALSE: MLE may be biased in small samples. (3) TRUE: Cramér-Rao lower bound. (4) TRUE: Sufficient statistic contains all information about θ.

answer.text: The maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) is asymptotically efficient under regularity conditions.
The variance of any unbiased estimator of \(\theta\) is at least \(1/I(\theta)\) (Cramér-Rao bound), where \(I(\theta)\) is Fisher information.
A sufficient statistic for \(\theta\) captures all information about \(\theta\) contained in the sample.

name.text: P02 Q52 MSQ - Countability and cardinality

questiontext.text: Which of the following sets have the same cardinality as \(\mathbb{R}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: |ℝ|=|(0,1)|=𝔠. (2) TRUE: |ℝ²|=|ℝ|=𝔠 (Cantor pairing). (3) FALSE: |ℤ|=ℵ₀ < 𝔠. (4) TRUE: |2^ℕ|=𝔠 (Cantor's theorem / continuum hypothesis).

answer.text: The set of all subsets of \(\mathbb{N}\) (power set \(2^{\mathbb{N}}\))

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about polynomial rings \(\mathbb{F}[x]\) where \(\mathbb{F}\) is a field?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: F[x] is a Euclidean domain (division algorithm). (2) TRUE: Every PID is a UFD. (3) TRUE: Maximal ideals in F[x] correspond to irreducible polynomials. (4) TRUE: F[x]/(p(x)) is a field iff p is irreducible.

answer.text: \(\mathbb{F}[x]\) is a Euclidean domain (the degree function is a Euclidean norm).
Every maximal ideal in \(\mathbb{F}[x]\) is generated by an irreducible polynomial.
\(\mathbb{F}[x]/(p(x))\) is a field if and only if \(p(x)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{F}\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about \(L^p\) spaces on \([0,1]\) with Lebesgue measure?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (2),(3),(4)

(1) FALSE: L²[0,1] ⊂ L¹[0,1] (for finite measure spaces, higher p gives smaller space). (2) TRUE: Hölder's inequality. (3) TRUE: L² is a Hilbert space. (4) TRUE: Minkowski's inequality (triangle inequality for Lp).

answer.text: For \(f\in L^p\) and \(g\in L^q\) with \(1/p+1/q=1\), we have \(fg\in L^1\) and \(\|fg\|_1\leq\|f\|_p\|g\|_q\) (Hölder's inequality).
\(L^2[0,1]\) is a Hilbert space with inner product \(\langle f,g\rangle=\int_0^1 f\bar{g}\).

questiontext.text: For the linear system \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\) where \(A\) is a constant \(n\times n\) matrix, which are TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Solution is x(t)=e^{At}x₀. (2) TRUE: Matrix exponential defined by series. (3) TRUE: If A=PDP⁻¹ then e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P⁻¹. (4) TRUE: The Wronskian of n linearly independent solutions is nonzero.

answer.text: The solution with initial condition \(\mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0\) is \(\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0\).
The matrix exponential \(e^{At}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty \frac{(At)^k}{k!}\) converges for all \(t\).
The Wronskian of \(n\) linearly independent solutions of \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\) is nonzero for all \(t\).
